zukunft.com.description: Evidence base on whether experiencing heatwaves and other extreme weather events shifts climate-change opinion, concern, pro-environmental behaviour, and support for climate policy. Central pattern: extreme weather tends to raise risk perception but rarely changes concern, behaviour, or policy support; effects are small, event-specific, and short-lived. What actually predicts policy support is perceived fairness and effectiveness, not experienced threat. All content in English per project convention. Person-neutral.

words.name: extreme weather experience
words.description: Direct personal exposure to an extreme weather event (heatwave, flood, fire, hurricane, severe storm) within a defined recent window before measurement.

words.name: heatwave
words.description: Period of unusually hot weather persisting at least two consecutive days, defined relative to local climatological conditions (WMO). One empirical definition used: at least three consecutive days above 29 degrees Celsius daytime maximum.

words.name: risk perception
words.description: A person's assessed likelihood and severity of climate-change impacts. Distinct from concern and from behaviour; can rise without either changing.

words.name: climate concern
words.description: Degree of worry or felt seriousness about climate change. An attitudinal measure distinct from risk perception and from behaviour.

words.name: pro-environmental behaviour
words.description: Self-reported or observed individual actions intended to reduce environmental or climate impact.

words.name: climate policy support
words.description: Stated support for climate taxes, laws, or the need for collective action. The outcome most relevant to a petition.

words.name: perceived fairness
words.description: Belief that a climate policy distributes costs and benefits justly. Identified as a top determinant of policy support.

words.name: perceived effectiveness
words.description: Belief that a climate policy will actually achieve its intended outcome. Identified as a top determinant of policy support.

words.name: attenuation over time
words.description: Tendency of any opinion effect from an extreme weather event to fade quickly after the event, rather than persist.

words.name: optimism bias for self
words.description: Pattern where people acknowledge rising heat risk for others while not seeing themselves as personally at risk, weakening personal motivation to act.

words.name: local warming effect
words.description: Short-term tendency to believe more in global warming on days that feel unusually warm; a transient, weather-dependent belief shift rather than a durable one.

words.name: compound exposure
words.description: Having experienced more than one type of extreme event (e.g. both flood and heatwave), which in one study did raise concern and behaviour, unlike single-event exposure.

words.name: climate disinformation reframing
words.description: Coordinated narratives that reinterpret heatwaves as natural or exaggerated, neutralising their potential persuasive value; commonly conflates short-term weather with long-term climate.

sources.name: Lohmann & Kontoleon 2023
sources.description: Lohmann, P. M., & Kontoleon, A. (2023). Do Flood and Heatwave Experiences Shape Climate Opinion? Causal Evidence from Flooding and Heatwaves in England and Wales. Environmental and Resource Economics, 86(1), 263-304. Difference-in-differences matching on geo-referenced panel data.

sources.name: Visconti et al. 2024
sources.description: Visconti, G., Young, K., & Weis Kintziger, K. (2024). The effect of different extreme weather events on attitudes toward climate change. PLOS ONE, 19(5), e0300967. Three-wave US panel, dynamic difference-in-differences. Tested fires, floods, hurricanes, severe storms (heatwaves not separately tested).

sources.name: Bergquist et al. 2022
sources.description: Bergquist, M., Nilsson, A., Harring, N., & Jagers, S. C. (2022). Meta-analyses of fifteen determinants of public opinion about climate change taxes and laws. Nature Climate Change, 12(3), 235-240. 89 datasets, 51 articles, 33 countries, N=119,465.

sources.name: Zaval et al. 2014
sources.description: Zaval, L., Keenan, E. A., Johnson, E. J., & Weber, E. U. (2014). How warm days increase belief in global warming. Nature Climate Change, 4(2), 143-147. Demonstrates transient, weather-dependent belief shifts (local-warming effect).

sources.name: Howarth et al. 2025
sources.description: Nationally representative UK survey (N=1750, summer 2023) on public perceptions of extreme heat, identifying gaps between acknowledged risk to others and perceived personal risk. Published in Climate Risk Management / related outlet.

sources.name: EDMO 2025
sources.description: European Digital Media Observatory (2025). The same old story - a heat wave of climate disinformation. Documents recurring narratives that reframe heatwaves as natural or exaggerated.

values.name: heatwave experience does not raise climate concern on average
values.notes_replaced_by_words: No average effect on concern. The exception is compound exposure: experiencing both flood and heatwave did raise concern and behaviour. See word 'compound exposure'.

values.name: heatwave experience does not raise pro-environmental behaviour on average
compound flood-and-heatwave exposure does raise concern and behaviour
heatwave risk-perception effect is driven by recent salient events
values.notes_replaced_by_words: Effect attributed to the salient 2018 and 2019 summer heatwaves, indicating recency-dependence rather than a stable shift. See word 'attenuation over time'.

values.name: only fire exposure shifts climate belief and policy support
triple: climate belief and need for action
values.notes_replaced_by_words: Of fires, floods, hurricanes and severe storms, only fire produced a small significant effect; the others produced none. Heatwaves were not separately tested in this study, so this is adjacent rather than direct evidence on heat.

values.name: flood, hurricane and severe storm exposure have no effect on opinion
triple: flood hurricane or severe storm exposure

values.name: perceived fairness is a top determinant of policy support
perceived effectiveness is a top determinant of policy support
knowledge and demographics weakly predict policy support
values.notes_replaced_by_words: Self-enhancement values and climate knowledge showed weak relationships; demographic variables weak or near zero. Implication: informing or alarming people is a weaker lever than fairness and effectiveness.

values.name: warm days transiently increase belief in global warming
values.notes_replaced_by_words: Belief tracks recent local temperature, indicating the shift is transient and weather-dependent rather than durable. Mechanistically explains why a heatwave's persuasive value fades once it ends.

values.name: people see heat risk for others but not themselves
heatwaves are actively reframed by disinformation
values.notes_replaced_by_words: Recurring narratives: scientists exaggerate, heatwaves always existed, no CO2 link. A common tactic conflates short-term weather with long-term climate. This actively neutralises the event's persuasive potential.

values.name: experienced heat is a weak lever for climate policy compared to perceived fairness and effectiveness
triple: experienced heat as a persuasion lever
values.notes_replaced_by_words: Synthesis across the measured findings: heat raises risk perception but not concern, behaviour or policy support; the strongest measured predictors of support are fairness and effectiveness. Held as an assumed value because it combines several studies into a strategic conclusion rather than resting on a single direct measurement.
